RULES:
```

1. You can either:

<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

- After the trade, my ratio is 24/8 = 3.
- I am giving up Y which I have in abundance (25) to get X which I have very little of (5).
- Conversely, RED is giving up X (which they have in abundance, 25) to get Y (which they have very little of, 5).
- This is a mutually beneficial trade based on comparative advantage. They value Y more than I do (or need it more), and I value X more than they do.
- 3 X for 1 Y seems like a fair starting point given their inventory disparity. They have 5x as much X as Y, so trading X for Y is logical for them. I have 5x as much Y as X, so trading Y for X is logical for me.

- After trade: I have 8+24=32. They have 22+6=28. Total is still 30.
- The trade moves resources from the player who has an excess of one type to the player who has an excess of the other. This is efficient.
- There is no reason to reject or counter-offer. A counter-offer might lead to a stalemate or a worse deal in the final round. Accepting secures the gain of 3 X for the cost of 1 Y.

Therefore, I will accept the trade.</reason>
